---
title: "Data Wrangling"
subtitle: "Learning R with Dr. Hu and His Friends Workshop Series"
author: "Yue Hu"
institute: "Department of Political Science, THU"

webr:
  packages:
    - drhur
    - dplyr
    - tidyr
---

{{< include _extensions/r-wasm/live/_knitr.qmd >}}

## Outline


1. Single-dataset wrangling
    - Explore
    - Organize
    - Filter
    - Modify
2. Cross-dataset wrangling
    - Direct merging
    - Index-based merging

::: {.fragment}

*Research question*: Drawing on the "factors shaping people's views of equality" example, what useful information does the sample dataset `wvs7` contain?

:::


## Data Exploration

- Data exploration: an initial look at an unfamiliar dataset's composition, structure, format, and content
- A follow-up to data input, and the first step of any data analysis
- The simplest method is "just look at the data" — call the object name directly

::: {.fragment}

```{webr}
#| label: glimpse

# Load the data
data("wvs7", package = "drhur")

# Inspect the data frame
wvs7
```

:::

---

::: {.callout-tip title="The `tibble` data frame" .fragment}

- `wvs7` is stored as a `tibble` — an upgraded version of the ordinary data frame
- It still has rows, columns, and cells
- Almost all functions that work on `data.frame` also work on `tibble`
- It is the standard format across the tidyverse toolchain

:::


- Direct inspection is convenient but unwieldy when data are large
- We need more systematic ways to examine structure and individual variables

## Structural Exploration

- Data structure = the size and composition of the data: number of observations, number of variables, variable names
- Probe functions usually carry abbreviated English names
- Example: `str` means "structure", listing each variable's type, row count, and column count

::: {.fragment}

```{webr}
#| label: systemView

library(dplyr)

# Number of rows
nrow(wvs7)

# Number of columns
ncol(wvs7)

# Variable names (first 6 only)
head(names(wvs7))

# The tidyverse-preferred structure inspector
glimpse(wvs7)
```

:::

---

- The data have 1264 rows and 24 columns
- `glimpse()` displays the dataset more compactly: column names listed vertically; each variable's type annotated (`<fct>` factor, `<dbl>` double, etc.); a preview of the first few values for each variable

## Extracting Variable Information

- Two ways to extract a variable:
    - Matrix-style brackets: `<df>[, "name"]`
    - `$` form: `<df>$<name>` (most common)

::: {.fragment}

```{webr}
#| label: variable_extract

# Bracket form (showing only the first 6 rows)
head(wvs7[, "country"])

# $ form (showing only the first 10 unique values)
unique(wvs7$country) |> head(10)
```

:::

---

- Numeric variables: `mean()`, `median()`, `max()`, `var()`, `summary()`, etc.

::: {.fragment}

```{webr}
#| label: varCal_numeric

# Mean age of respondents
mean(wvs7$age, na.rm = TRUE)

# Distribution of income level
summary(wvs7$incomeLevel)
```

:::

---

- Ordinal and categorical variables: `unique()` for the value set, `table()` for the frequency distribution

::: {.fragment}

```{webr}
#| label: varCal_categorical

# Frequency distribution of age
table(wvs7$age)

# Categorical breakdown of gender
table(wvs7$female)

# Categorical breakdown of marital status
table(wvs7$marital)
```

:::

- `table()` returns different output for different variable types — yet another instance of OOP polymorphism

## Data Organization {.smaller}

- Data organization = purposefully picking variables, filtering observations, and reshaping how the data are presented
- Base R has its own functions; `tidyverse` makes the operations more intuitive
- Mainstay: `dplyr` (data cleaning) plus parts of `tidyr`


- `tidyverse` is a meta-package — a collection of R packages
- Members include `tidyr`, `dplyr`, `stringr`, `tibble`, etc.
- They share a common data structure and design philosophy (the "tidy universe")
- `library(tidyverse)` loads the core members in one shot


- `dplyr` specializes in data cleaning
- Goal: reshape raw data into an analysis-ready form
- Includes adjustments to variable names, types, and value encodings


- The pipe: hands the result of one command on to the next function
- R has a native pipe `|>`; once `dplyr` is loaded, `%>%` is also available
- Analogous to chaining a combo in an action game: commands line up in execution order and read easily

---

- Traditional nested style (read inside-out, hard to follow):

```r
# Making dumplings, expressed as nested calls
eatDumpling <- eat(
  dip(
    cook(
      fill(
        mix(meat, with = c(salt, soy_sauce, green_onion, ginger)),
        with = wrapper
      ),
      in_water = boiled_water
    ),
    in_sauce = vinegar
  )
)
```

---

- Pipe style (read top-down, logic is clear):

```r
# Making dumplings, expressed as a pipe
eatDumpling_pipe <- mix(meat, with = c(salt, soy_sauce, green_onion, ginger)) |>
  fill(with = wrapper) |>
  cook(in_water = boiled_water) |>
  dip(in_sauce = vinegar) |>
  eat()
```

---

- Read `|>` as "then" or "and then" — the function chain becomes close to natural language


::: {.callout-note title="Pipe shortcut" .fragment}

- Windows / Linux: `Ctrl + Shift + M`
- Mac: `Cmd + Shift + M`

:::


- Four common wrangling operations: select, arrange, modify, summarize

## Selecting Variables

- Picking columns vs. picking rows
- For columns: use `select()`
- Syntax: `select(<df>, <var1>, <var2>, ...)`

::: {.fragment}

```{webr}
#| label: select

library(dplyr)

# Pick five variables of interest
select(wvs7, equalIncentive, incomeLevel, confidence_gov, education, country)
```

:::

---

- Pattern-based bulk selection:
    - `starts_with()`: starts with a given string
    - `ends_with()`: ends with a given string
    - `matches()`: regular-expression match anywhere in the name

::: {.fragment}

```{webr}
#| label: select_startwith

# All variables beginning with "corruption"
select(wvs7, starts_with("corruption"))
```

:::

::: {.callout-tip title="Pattern-based selection helpers" .fragment}

- `starts_with`: opening characters
- `ends_with`: closing characters
- `matches`: regular-expression match — can target any position in the name
- Note: third-person singular `s` — easy to forget

:::

---

- Drop variables by prepending `-`
- `select(wvs7, -corruption_state)` drops a single variable
- `select(wvs7, -(varA:varB))` drops a contiguous range
- Can be combined with helpers such as `starts_with`

::: {.fragment}

```{webr}
#| label: deselect

# Pick all corruption variables, then drop the country-level one
select(wvs7, starts_with("corruption"), -corruption_state)
```

:::

---

- `rename()` changes a variable name
- Syntax: `rename(<df>, <new_name> = <old_name>)`
- Multiple variables can be renamed at once

::: {.fragment}

```{webr}
#| label: rename

# Rename 'country' to 'nationality'
rename(wvs7, nationality = country)
```

:::

---


- Note: `rename()` only prints a preview — it does not overwrite the original data frame
- To make the change permanent, assign back with `<-`
- Example: `wvs7 <- rename(wvs7, nationality = country)`

## Filtering Rows

- `filter()` keeps rows based on variable values
- Syntax: `filter(<df>, <condition1>, <condition2>, ...)`
- Multiple conditions separated by commas — equivalent to logical AND

::: {.fragment}

```{webr}
#| label: filter

# Single condition: keep South Korean respondents only
select(wvs7, equalIncentive, country) |>
  filter(country == "South Korea")

# Set condition: South Korea or Japan
select(wvs7, equalIncentive, country) |>
  filter(country %in% c("South Korea", "Japan"))
```

:::

---

- `filter()` supports any logical or comparison expression
- Compound example: Japanese respondents over age 50

::: {.fragment}

```{webr}
#| label: filter_compound

# Two conditions at once
select(wvs7, equalIncentive, country, age) |>
  filter(country == "Japan", age > 50)
```

:::

::: {.callout-note title="Why didn't the rename stick?" .fragment}

- `rename()` does not automatically overwrite the data in your environment
- To make the change permanent, you must assign back with `<-`
- Otherwise, the original variable name is still there when you use `wvs7` next

:::

## Arranging Rows

- `arrange()` sorts rows by variable values
- Syntax: `arrange(<df>, <var1>, <var2>, ...)`
- Default order is ascending (smallest to largest)

::: {.fragment}

```{webr}
#| label: arrange

# Sort by income level (low to high)
select(wvs7, equalIncentive, incomeLevel, country) |>
  filter(country == "Japan") |>
  arrange(incomeLevel)
```

:::

---

- Two ways to do descending:
    - Wrap the variable in `desc()`
    - Prepend a minus sign `-` (numeric variables only)

::: {.fragment}

```{webr}
#| label: arrange_desc

# Method 1: desc() helper
select(wvs7, equalIncentive, incomeLevel, country) |>
  filter(country == "Japan") |>
  arrange(desc(incomeLevel))

# Method 2: minus sign (numeric only)
select(wvs7, equalIncentive, incomeLevel, country) |>
  filter(country == "Japan") |>
  arrange(-incomeLevel)
```

:::

---

- `arrange()` also accepts multiple sort keys
- Order determines priority: rows are sorted by the first variable; ties go to the second

::: {.fragment}

```{webr}
#| label: arrange_compound

# Sort by income first; ties go to age
select(wvs7, equalIncentive, incomeLevel, age) |>
  arrange(incomeLevel, age)
```

:::

---

::: {.callout-warning title="Order of sort keys" .fragment}

- Argument order in `select` and `filter` does not matter
- In `arrange`, **argument order determines sort priority**
- Swapping the keys yields a completely different result

:::

::: {.fragment}

```{webr}
#| label: arrange_order

# Sort by age first, then by income
select(wvs7, equalIncentive, incomeLevel, age) |>
  arrange(age, incomeLevel)
```

:::

## Modifying Variables

- Reshape how a variable is encoded
- Example: collapse a 10-level income variable into a "poor / rich" binary
- Use `mutate()` — syntax: `mutate(<df>, <new_var> = <expression>)`

::: {.fragment}

```{webr}
#| label: mutate

# New variable poor: TRUE if income <= 3
select(wvs7, equalIncentive, incomeLevel) |>
  mutate(poor = incomeLevel <= 3)
```

:::

---

- `mutate()` can create several new variables at once, separated by `,`
- Multiple new variables can be derived from the same source

::: {.fragment}

```{webr}
#| label: mutate_multiple

# Create poor and rich in one call
select(wvs7, equalIncentive, incomeLevel) |>
  mutate(
    poor = incomeLevel <= 3,
    rich = incomeLevel >= 6
  )
```

:::

---

::: {.callout-warning title="Beware of duplicate names" .fragment}

- `mutate` allows the same name to be assigned twice — the later assignment overwrites the earlier
- In one pipeline, giving the same name two different expressions causes the first to be discarded

:::

::: {.fragment}

```{webr}
#| label: mutate_overwrite

# poor is defined twice; the second definition wins
select(wvs7, country, incomeLevel) |>
  mutate(
    poor = incomeLevel <= 3,
    poor = incomeLevel <= 5
  )
```

:::

## Direct Merging {.smaller}

- Data merging: combine multiple data frames into a single new one
- Single-source data carry limited information; multi-source merging amplifies analytical value
- Two paths: direct merging and index-based merging
- Direct merging: stack same-structured data horizontally or vertically
- Two flavors:
    - Row-binding: equal column count required; `bind_rows()`
    - Column-binding: equal row count required; `bind_cols()`

::: {.fragment}

```{webr}
#| label: bindRow

# Mock data 1: first 3 South-Korean rows
wvs7_sk3 <- filter(wvs7, country == "South Korea") |>
  head(n = 3)

# Mock data 2: first 3 Japanese rows
wvs7_ja3 <- filter(wvs7, country == "Japan") |>
  head(n = 3)

# Row-bind
bind_rows(wvs7_sk3, wvs7_ja3)
```

:::

---

::: {.callout-warning title="Row-binding with unequal columns" .fragment}

- `bind_rows` still works when column counts disagree
- Missing cells are automatically filled with `NA`
- Useful when sources have slightly different structures

:::

::: {.fragment}

```{webr}
#| label: bindRow_unequal

# Data 1: income and country only
wvs7_sk3_income <- select(wvs7, incomeLevel, country) |>
  filter(country == "South Korea") |>
  head(n = 3)

# Data 2: country and government confidence only
wvs7_ja3_confidence <- select(wvs7, country, confidence_gov) |>
  filter(country == "Japan") |>
  head(n = 3)

# Unequal-column row-binding: mismatched cells become NA
bind_rows(wvs7_sk3_income, wvs7_ja3_confidence)
```

:::

---

- Column-binding: use `bind_cols`
- The function name is plural — do not forget the "s"

::: {.fragment}

```{webr}
#| label: bindCols

# Prep 1: all confidence variables
wvs7_confidence <- select(wvs7, starts_with("confidence"))

# Prep 2: country variable
wvs7_country <- select(wvs7, country)

# Column-bind
bind_cols(wvs7_confidence, wvs7_country)
```

:::

## Index-Based Merging

- Combine datasets by matching index values
- Safer than direct merging: as long as the index variables share a structure, the merge stays clean
- Supports one-to-one and one-to-many


::: {.callout-important title="Structural consistency" .fragment}

- Structural consistency = the index variables follow the same coding scheme
- The variables need not cover every possible value
- Example: with "gender" as the index, as long as 0 = male, 1 = female stays consistent, the merge works correctly

:::

---

- Demonstration data:
    - `wvs7_eq`: individual-level, Japan + South Korea
    - `country_info`: country-level, Japan + Thailand
- Both have a country variable serving as index (under different names)

::: {.fragment}

```{webr}
#| label: join_data

# Data 1: individual-level data for Japan and South Korea
wvs7_eq <- select(wvs7, country, equalIncentive, female, education) |>
  filter(country %in% c("Japan", "South Korea"))

# Data 2: country-level macro data for Japan and Thailand
country_info <- filter(wvs7, country %in% c("Japan", "Thailand")) |>
  group_by(country) |>
  summarise(across(female:education, \(x) mean(x, na.rm = TRUE))) |>
  ungroup() |>
  rename(nation = country)

# Inspect
wvs7_eq
country_info
```

:::

---

::: {style="font-size: 0.7em;"}

- `dplyr` offers four index-based joins:
    - **Left join `left_join`**: keep all rows of the left table; merge in the right where keys match; right-only keys are dropped
    - **Right join `right_join`**: mirror of the left join
    - **Inner join `inner_join`**: keep only keys present in both tables (intersection)
    - **Full join `full_join`**: keep all keys from either side (union); unmatched cells become `NA`
- Generic syntax: `<type>_join(<data1>, <data2>, by = join_by(<index>))`

:::

::: {.fragment}

```{webr}
#| label: join

# Run all four join types
wvs7_lj <- left_join(wvs7_eq, country_info, by = join_by(country == nation))
wvs7_rj <- right_join(wvs7_eq, country_info, by = join_by(country == nation))
wvs7_ij <- inner_join(wvs7_eq, country_info, by = join_by(country == nation))
wvs7_fj <- full_join(wvs7_eq, country_info, by = join_by(country == nation))

# Compare which countries each join retained
wvs7_lj$country |> unique()
wvs7_rj$country |> unique()
wvs7_ij$country |> unique()
wvs7_fj$country |> unique()
```

:::

## Exercise {.unnumbered background-color="#43464B"}

**Question 1**: Comprehensive data wrangling

On `wvs7`, write a single `dplyr` pipeline that does all of the following:

1. Create a new variable `avg_confidence`: the arithmetic mean of `confidence_gov` and `confidence_court`
2. Filter to keep only respondents who satisfy:
    - `country` equals `"Japan"`
    - `age` greater than 40 and less than 60
    - `avg_confidence` greater than 3
3. Sort the result by `avg_confidence` in descending order
4. Keep only the four variables `age`, `incomeLevel`, `equalIncentive`, `avg_confidence`

::: { .hint exercise="dplyr_pipeline" }

- Use `mutate()` to add the new variable
- Use `filter()` with multiple conditions
- Use `arrange(desc(...))` for descending order
- Use `select()` to keep only the specified variables
- Order: mutate → filter → arrange → select

:::

::: {.fragment}

```{webr}
#| label: dplyr_pipeline
#| exercise: dplyr_pipeline

library(dplyr)
data("wvs7", package = "drhur")

# Build the dplyr pipeline here

```

:::

::: { .solution exercise="dplyr_pipeline" }

```r
library(dplyr)
data("wvs7", package = "drhur")

wvs7 |>
  mutate(avg_confidence = (confidence_gov + confidence_court) / 2) |>
  filter(
    country == "Japan",
    age > 40,
    age < 60,
    avg_confidence > 3
  ) |>
  arrange(desc(avg_confidence)) |>
  select(age, incomeLevel, equalIncentive, avg_confidence)

# Explanation:
# 1. mutate: create avg_confidence (mean of confidence in government and courts)
# 2. filter: three conditions -- Japan, 40 < age < 60, avg_confidence > 3
# 3. arrange(desc(...)): sort by avg_confidence from high to low
# 4. select: keep only the four target variables
```

:::

## Recap {.smaller}

:::: {.columns}

::: {.column width="50%"}

- Data exploration
    - Row / column count: `nrow()` / `ncol()`
    - Data structure: `glimpse()`
    - Variable names: `names()`
    - Unique values: `unique()`
    - Frequency distribution: `table()`
- Data wrangling
    - Pipe: `|>`
    - Select columns: `select()`
    - Rename: `rename()`
    - Filter rows: `filter()`
    - Arrange: `arrange()`
    - Add / modify: `mutate()`

:::

::: {.column width="50%"}

- Data merging
    - Row-bind: `bind_rows()`
    - Column-bind: `bind_cols()`
    - Left join: `left_join()`
    - Right join: `right_join()`
    - Inner join: `inner_join()`
    - Full join: `full_join()`

:::

::::
